headline: Washington forced Anthropic to yank its two newest AI models worldwide as an Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day drained universities and more than 400 Arch Linux packages turned into malware.

emerging_trends.subject: AI Weaponized
emerging_trends.text: Criminals bent Google's Gemini into a smishing engine while regulators yanked Anthropic's frontier models over a jailbreak, showing AI as both attack tool and policy flashpoint.

emerging_trends.subject: Supply Chain
emerging_trends.text: Hijacked Arch Linux packages, an Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day, and a decade-old phpBB flaw all turned trusted software into the entry point.

emerging_trends.subject: Deep Persistence
emerging_trends.text: Velvet Ant hid inside Linux login components for nearly ten years and phpBB carried an auth bypass for a decade, rewarding attackers who plant access where cleanup never reaches.

emerging_trends.subject: Breach Penalties
emerging_trends.text: South Korea's record $409 million Coupang fine and a $47 million 23andMe settlement fund mark a steep financial reckoning for companies that lose customer data.

topics.title: U.S. Pulls Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5
topics.last_24h: Anthropic disabled Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for every user worldwide on June 13 after a federal order barred foreign nationals from the models.
topics.summary: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic at 5:21 p.m. ET to block all foreign nationals, inside or outside the country, from its two most advanced models, citing national security and a specific jailbreak. Anthropic complied by taking both offline globally and disputes the basis, calling the jailbreak narrow and the underlying capability widely available elsewhere. Dark Reading frames Mythos 5 as an upgrade over the preview and Fable 5 as Mythos made safe for general use. The suspension strands paying customers everywhere while the company contests the directive.

sources.title: US Gov asks Anthropic to ban 'foreign national' access to Fable, Mythos
U.S. Orders Anthropic to Suspend Fable 5 and Mythos 5 Access for Foreign Nationals
Anthropic Says It Has Taken Its Latest AI Models Offline to Comply With New Export Controls
Claude Fable 5 Doesn't Change the Mythos Security Story

topics.title: Google Sues Chinese Gemini-Powered Smishing Network
topics.last_24h: Google filed suit on June 12 against Outsider Enterprise, a China-based network it accuses of weaponizing Gemini to flood Americans with phishing texts.
topics.summary: Google alleges the group runs a phishing-as-a-service kit called Outsider and used its Gemini AI agent to generate convincing scam infrastructure. The operation has hit hundreds of thousands of victims, caused losses in the millions, and ties to more than 9,000 fake sites and a million fraudulent URLs. Google's lawsuit aims to dismantle the infrastructure and signals a pattern of criminals using AI to make fraud more believable. Defenders should expect higher-quality smishing lures and tune filtering accordingly.

sources.title: Google Sues Chinese Smishing Network Accused of Using Gemini AI in Phishing
Google sues China-based scammers over Gemini AI abuse

topics.title: Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day Loots Universities
topics.last_24h: Researchers disclosed a critical PeopleSoft zero-day that ShinyHunters exploited to steal gigabytes from hundreds of organizations, hitting American universities hardest.
topics.summary: A maximum-severity flaw in Oracle-owned PeopleSoft enterprise software let attackers reach into ERP deployments and pull out large volumes of data. The ShinyHunters extortion crew capitalized fast, disproportionately compromising U.S. higher education, which leans heavily on the platform for student and financial records. Affected institutions should patch immediately, hunt for signs of bulk data export, and assume credential exposure. Ars Technica rates the bug about as critical as vulnerabilities come.

sources.title: ShinyHunters Uses Oracle Zero-Day to Rampage Higher Ed
PeopleSoft 0-day affecting hundreds of organizations steals gigabytes of data

topics.title: More Than 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked
topics.last_24h: Attackers seized over 400 packages in the Arch User Repository this week and rewrote their build scripts to drop a credential stealer and rootkit.
topics.summary: The Arch User Repository, Arch Linux's community-maintained package collection, saw more than 400 entries weaponized to install a Rust infostealer on any machine that built them. The malware harvests developer secrets and access tokens, and when it runs as root it loads an eBPF rootkit to hide itself. Developers who built affected packages should rotate every credential and API key, rebuild from clean sources, and inspect for kernel-level persistence. The incident underscores how community package channels carry trust that attackers can abuse at scale.

sources.title: Over 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked to Deploy Infostealer and eBPF Rootkit
Over 400 Arch Linux packages compromised to push rootkit, infostealer

topics.title: Velvet Ant Backdoors Linux Login for Nearly a Decade
topics.last_24h: Sygnia revealed that the China-nexus group Velvet Ant hid inside a target's Linux login system for close to ten years.
topics.summary: Rather than camping on closely watched laptops and servers, Velvet Ant backdoored the PAM and OpenSSH components that decide who may sign in, planting access where routine cleanup never looked. Sygnia, which tracks the group, found the implants survived because defenders rarely audit the authentication layer itself. Organizations should verify the integrity of PAM modules and SSH binaries, watch for unexplained authentication paths, and treat long-lived Linux infrastructure as a prime persistence target. The case shows nation-state crews investing in dwell time measured in years.

sources.title: China-Linked Hackers Backdoored Linux Login Software to Hide for Nearly a Decade

topics.title: Record Breach Penalties Hit Coupang and 23andMe
topics.last_24h: South Korea fined Coupang a record $409 million over a data breach the same day a bankruptcy administrator approved a $47 million fund for 23andMe breach victims.
topics.summary: South Korea's privacy commission levied its largest-ever data-breach penalty against e-commerce giant Coupang, surpassing the $88.8 million it fined SK Telecom earlier this year. Separately, a U.S. bankruptcy administrator approved a $47 million settlement for roughly 7 million 23andMe customers whose genetic data hackers stole starting in April 2023 and posted on the dark web. Together the actions show regulators and courts attaching real money to lost customer data. Companies holding sensitive personal records face mounting financial exposure when controls fail.

sources.title: South Korea hits Coupang with record $409 million fine over data breach
Bankruptcy admin approves settlement fund of $47 million for 23andMe data breach victims

topics.title: Section 702 Surveillance Program Set to Lapse
topics.last_24h: Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act is poised to expire after a legislative deadlock, its first lapse since 2008.
topics.summary: Section 702 authorizes a major U.S. foreign-intelligence collection program, and congressional gridlock now threatens to let it expire for the first time in its history. Intelligence agencies rely on the authority to gather communications of foreign targets, and a lapse forces a scramble over how ongoing collection continues. Privacy advocates and surveillance hawks have long fought over reauthorization terms, and the deadlock leaves the program's future unsettled. Organizations tied to federal intelligence sharing should track how a gap affects threat reporting.

sources.title: Major US surveillance program poised to lapse after legislative deadlock

business_politics.text: SpaceX raised $75 billion in the largest initial public offering ever, and shares jumped nearly a fifth on debut to value the rocket and AI company at $2.1 trillion, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire.

reading.author: Ed Zitron
reading.title: Premium: The Silicon Valley Bubble (Part 1)
reading.summary: Zitron argues the AI era is ending, pointing to OpenAI and Anthropic filing to go public as a race for exit liquidity by two companies that burn billions a year with no path to profit.

reading.author: Stratechery
reading.title: Fable 5, Anthropic Alignment, AI Tiers
reading.summary: Thompson calls Fable 5, the public version of Mythos, very capable while warning that its release sets troubling new precedents for alignment and tiered access to AI models.

reading.author: Cal Newport
reading.title: Why Isn't AI Taking Our Jobs?
reading.summary: Newport examines why AI has so far failed to eliminate work the way industrial machines replaced manual labor, questioning the brawn-to-brains analogy that AI executives favor.
